content.problemStatement: Chef likes to play with big numbers. Today, he has a big positive integer **N**. He can select any two digits from this number (the digits can be same but their positions should be different) and orders them in any one of the two possible ways. For each of these ways, he creates a two digit number from it (might contain leading zeros). Then, he will pick a character corresponding to the ASCII value equal to this number, i.e. the number 65 corresponds to 'A', 66 to 'B' and so on till 90 for 'Z'. Chef is only interested in finding which of the characters in the range 'A' to 'Z' can possibly be picked this way.
constraints: N consists only of decimal digits '0'‑'9'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single positive integer N (given as a decimal string, length up to 10^7).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing all uppercase letters (from 'A' to 'Z') that can be obtained, sorted in increasing alphabetical order. If no letter can be obtained, output an empty line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each N we count digit frequencies and check every ASCII code from 65 to 90. The resulting letters are listed in alphabetical order.
N contains only one digit, so no pair of distinct positions exists. Hence no character can be formed.
Each digit appears at least twice, therefore every two‑digit number from 65 to 90 can be built, giving all letters A‑Z.

solution.approach: For each test case we only need the count of each digit (0‑9). A two‑digit number X (65 ≤ X ≤ 90) can be formed if its tens digit a and units digit b both appear in N and either a ≠ b (different positions are guaranteed) or a == b and the digit occurs at least twice. We compute the digit frequencies in O(|N|) time, then iterate X from 65 to 90 and test the above condition, appending the corresponding character when possible. This yields O(|N| + 26) time and O(1) extra memory per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|N|) per test case (linear scan of the string).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a 10‑element frequency array and a few variables.
